Then we see that the first person wants to get as much as they can, but also wants the part to seem just. The second person can get a little if they say yes, but can also say no and get nothing if they think the part is not just. When we look at what people really do, we see they often say no to very small asks, even though that means they get nothing, which shows that the idea of just can be bigger than just getting money. Some might say that people only care about getting money, but the many times we see people say no to small asks show that the other side is true. So the best answer is that the share offer game shows that the idea of what is just matters a lot for how people act and any view of how people play game should have that idea. The share offer game shows that the idea of just changes choices, so a view of how people play in real life should have that idea.

Even so, in most cases, things like the middle of the day or a big building are so clear that most people agree on them. So, the idea holds up well for many situations. Finally, we see that while he breaks the good thinking ways, his feelings explain what he does. It is a human way of being. The person acts like his own food is special and bought food is not. This shows how people keep things in different boxes in their heads even when they are the same.

english_query: If the bread has risen, then the yeast was alive. If the yeast was alive, then driving is dangerous. Therefore, if the bread has risen, then driving is dangerous. Is this argument valid or invalid? Explain your reasoning.
ugf_response: First we see a line that says if the food has rise then the small living stuff that makes food rise was living. Next we see a line that says if the small living stuff that makes food rise was living then drive is bad. Then we can join the two lines: the thing that makes the food rise also makes drive bad. So if the food has rise the small living stuff was living and that means drive bad. That makes the last line true. follows

english_query: During the harvest season, the player, a newly appointed village elder, must decide how to allocate the upcoming grain bounty. The village council presents two pairs of allocation plans, each involving uncertain weather outcomes.
ugf_response: First, we look at the first pair of plans. In both of these, the plan for good sky gives the most food, so a person who thinks well would pick that one to help the most people. Then we look at the second pair. In both of these, the plan for bad sky saves more lives if things go wrong, so a person who thinks well would pick that one to keep people safe. But here is the trouble. The good sky plan in the first pair is the same as the good sky plan in the second pair. And the bad sky plan in the first pair is the same as the bad sky plan in the second pair. So, if you pick the top choice in the first group, you are picking the same thing as the bottom choice in the second group. This means you cannot think well in both groups at the same time. You have to choose what matters more: hoping for the best or being safe from the worst. There is no single answer that wins in every way because the two groups push you in the other direction. The player faces a choice between trying to get the most good or trying to stop the most bad. Since the plans are the same, the player must decide if they care more about the best possible end or the safest possible end, and stick to that choice.

english_query: Either she will take the train or she will take the bus. She will not take the train. Therefore, she will take the bus. Is this argument valid or invalid? Explain your reasoning.
ugf_response: First, we look at the first idea. It says one of two things must happen. Either she goes on the train, or she goes on the bus. This means she has to pick one. She cannot do both, and she cannot do none. Next, we look at the second idea. It tells us she will not take the train. This takes away the first choice. Now we see that the train is not her way. Since the first idea said she must take one or the other, and we know she is not taking the train, the only thing left is the bus. If she does not take the train, she has to take the bus. There is no other way for the first idea to be true. So, if we agree with the first two ideas, we must also agree with the last idea. It is not possible to say she does not take the bus while also saying she does not take the train and that she must take one of them. That would break the first idea. So the last idea is true because of the first two. follows

english_query: If he turns on the light, then the room is bright. The room is not bright. Therefore, he does not turn on the light. Is this argument valid or invalid? Explain your reasoning.
ugf_response: First, we look at the first idea, which says that if he turns on the light, then the room is bright. This sets up a clear tie between the light and the light in the room. Then, the second idea tells us that the room is not bright. We need to see if this means he did not turn on the light. If he had turned on the light, the room would have to be bright, but we know it is not bright. So, he could not have turned on the light. This means the last idea follows for sure from the first two ideas. follows

english_query: Either the runner finished first or the runner finished second. The runner did not finish first. Therefore, the runner finished second. Is this argument valid or invalid? Explain your reasoning.
ugf_response: First, we look at the first idea. It says one of two things must be true. Either the runner came in first place, or the runner came in second place. There are no other ways this can be. Next, we look at the second idea. It tells us that the runner did not come in first place. This means the first choice is not true. Since one of the two choices had to be true, and the first one is out, the second choice must be the one that is true. So, the runner must have come in second place. We can try to see if this makes sense by thinking of a small story. Imagine the first two ideas are right. Can the last idea be wrong? If the runner did not come first, and the only other way was second, then saying the runner did not come second would mean the runner came in some other place, like third. But the first idea said only first or second were possible. So that story cannot happen. The last idea has to be right if the first two are right. So this means the last idea follows for sure. follows
